text: We're so excited to have you here today.
You could say you're Silicon Valley Story began here at Stanford GSB.
How does it feel to be back or at all began?
Kind of intimidating.
Honestly, I remember sitting in your shoes 25, 26 years ago and going to view from the top
in the old Bishop auditorium at the old campus and feeling in all of being in this incredible place.
It was just an amazing privilege to come to Stanford honestly.
As an international student, we obviously arrived earlier and so you could walk around campus.
It was still quiet and I had to pinch myself to realize that I was here.
I want to start where it all begins in South Africa.
Your grandfather, Paik Boetha, was a minister in Nelson Mandela's cabinet.
How did your family and where you grew up shape your career and your ambition?
In some things, having had a famous grandfather, my grandfather was sort of the equivalent of foreign secretary for many years in South Africa.
And I remember meeting dignitaries from overseas who'd come visit him.
He was prominent. He was a famous figure.
It meant that I was prejudged often. People would assume things about me because they inferred from whom I grant for the was or what he believed in.
And assumed that that was me.
That didn't sit well even though it was very proud of him.
I wanted to be myself.
But also gave me a sense of what you could achieve and what was possible in the world stage.
He had a book in his library, called The Who's Who.
And it was obviously before the internet.
It was this book, maybe had 1500 pages and they'd listed the names of all the important people in the world.
You know, names you needed to know.
And I remember seeing that when I was maybe 13 or 14 and wondering if I could maybe make my way into that book one day.
Supportive becoming a Who's Who leads you to Stanford.
And a Stanford you graduate as a Henry Ford scholar.
In other words, you were the number one student in your business school class.
What drove you to work so hard?
Because it was there.
The challenge existed.
I'm intensely curious.
I'm very competitive in driven.
And honestly, as an immigrant, I mean many of the students that I here presumably are also immigrant students.
You know, I got here on a two year student visa with a one year training certificate to be able to make way in the United States.
And so that is an incredible gift and an incredible opportunity.
And I didn't want to waste that.
And so that meant that I wanted to take on the challenge of getting the best grades I could.
And I didn't, I didn't aspire to be the top student when I got to business school, but at some point you started to form well.
And obviously, I'll be honest in the last semester when I was starting to work part time for PayPal actually took a few classes.
Past fail because I was worried about juggling work and studies.
So I had a bit of a glad in path in the last quarter.
Solid met that.
You clearly found a spot in the classroom.
And I think a lot of us wonder, what lessons are we going to take from the classroom into our lives?
What lessons did you take from the classroom in your career?
There are many.
And I think there was the being spent was the dean when I was here.
And part of what he won and about four was around the information theory.
So there's a lot of signal value in the fact that you have a Stanford MBA or a Stanford degree obviously.
And there's an element of branding. There's an element of the network that people you get to know here and that's invaluable.
The world's a small place.
Silicon Valley is an incredibly small place.
And one of the taglines I have is, friends come and go, enemies accumulate.
And if you look around this auditorium, if you look around your classroom at the GSB, there's going to be lots of your friends or colleagues or classmates that do really interesting things.
And you should delight in that.
And take pleasure in their success.
But the third element of the GSB is what you learn.
And I've gotten the sense that that has decreased in relative importance in the minds of students of the years and that concerns me honestly.
Because there is so much that you get to learn from these incredible teachers here at the GSB that can help you be effective when you get out there.
So I'm going to give you a couple of examples.
I remember in first year we did managing in non-market environments.
And as an immigrant, it just baffled me that we had to learn about this.
I was at business school while we were learning about these things.
And then as soon as I joined PayPal within six months we realized that eBay was engaging in what we considered to be unfame market practices.
And so we had a complete anti-trust case ready to go against eBay depending on how they behaved.
And so here I was immediately applying things that I had learned at the GSB.
Another one was cost accounting.
A class that I think few people would drawn to and yet in the first three months that I joined PayPal, what I was doing was a cost accounting exercise.
I was going through the company's actual financial statements,
understanding every single line what the variables were that drove it and building the company's financial model bottoms up.
And that model was still being used by eBay two years after the company was acquired.
So there's just so many practical things that you can learn in your classroom.
So I know that you hear to meet a lot of people and enjoy this experience.
Many of you are going to pivot your careers but take the time to learn.
There's so much valuable information you can get from your classroom.
But maybe the most important thing that came out of Stanford was actually your relationship with your wife,
Hoy Finn, who's actually in the audience.
What does today?
What influence has she had on you?
First I should tell you a little bit of story.
You saw some pictures earlier.
Of that we started dating in first year here at the GSB.
I think there was a picture of us in Schwab.
So some of that other residents that are now available weren't here.
But Schwab was newly constructed.
I think we were the second class to be in Schwab at the time.
I was in E 307.
Is it in your own my room?
So that picture was in E 307.
So it was a class of...
Hoy Finn was on the blue wing, the West Wing.
So it was a classic East End Boys, West End Girls, that was a story.
And I remember we had a human resource management class.
It must be in the spring semester.
And I try to figure out how I could sit in the back of the classroom at a sufficient angle that I could.
I could look at her without looking like I'm not actually paying attention to the difference.
So anyway, in addition to being my life partner, she's been my thought partner.
You know, whether it was helping me with initial decision to join PayPal.
I come to the US with some financial support from McKinsey.
So I owed them money back.
That paid for everything at the GSB but for most of it.
So I've not been changing to go back to them.
I had an offer to go to Goldman Sachs.
And I thought I wanted to go into finance.
So actually before I go to the GSB, I was doing sort of derivatives work.
That's what I thought I would do.
And none of the finance jobs I interviewed for made me an offer.
And then there was this idea of joining a startup.
And PayPal was the company that I'd sit with my sights on.
I thought it was really interesting.
But I was scared to join the startup.
You know, what would happen?
And Huyfen was also an immigrant.
She came from Singapore to United States and studied electrical and computer engineering at Carnegie Mellon University.
And she'd actually worked in startups before.
And so she had a little sense for what it would take.
And as we discussed this offer from this company, she ended up encouraging me to make that leap.
And I think I sometimes second-guessed it.
There were dog days at PayPal later that year.
And maybe sometimes it didn't appreciate how great her advice was.
But she was a huge influence in me making that first decision.
When I got to Sequoia and I got the offer, Mike Meritz and Doug Leone gave me a different offer to join.
And they said, you need to sign on the spot.
And I said, I can't.
I need to make a phone call.
We were engaged at the time.
But I insisted that I step out.
And obviously we'd been talking about this offer all along the interview process.
But I went to speak to her before accepting that offer.
So those are two examples.
And then throughout my career as an investor, having a partner who is technical, worked in startups, and
It's just been incredible to be able to talk about companies and about challenges.
There was one example of a company that there was a management strife.
The company wasn't perfectly managed.
The founders actually wanted to sell the company and we ran an M&A process.
But all the choirs could see that things weren't quite right.
And the best offer we'd get for the company was about $600 million.
And we didn't think it was a great offer.
And I was talking to her from about this.
And she had this absolutely perfect line for it, which is,
nobody wants to buy a fixer upper.
And it perfectly summarized the challenge of trying to sell a company with things on quite right.
And with that insight, we decided to turn down that acquisition offer.
And today that's a public company worth nearly $10 billion.
It was a good decision to turn that down.
So anyway, it's been wonderful to have somebody I met as a GSB both via a life and a thought partner.
That's a really special relationship that you have.
And what's also special is your both immigrants.
And the class of 2026, the GSB, is actually the most international in history.
Over 40% of the class comes from abroad.
Your famous recording Hamilton.
Immigrants, we get the job done.
What was that transition to the United States like?
And what challenges did you face along the way?
Why did you learn how to drive on the other side of the road?
And then there was, there was other little quirks with language.
So in South Africa, I had belonged to something called the A.A.
And I was in Schwab.
There was an American Dan Rapper port.
He actually moved into Schwab a little bit of other American students.
And I was chatting with him and I said, there's some, where do I sign up for the A.A.?
And he looked at me and he's like, oh my god, this guy's just...
He's sharing things with me.
And I said, the automobile association, somebody helps you with your car breaks down.
He said, ah, here we call it the triple A.
So these were some little adjustments.
The fact that I couldn't find an electric kettle anyway to boil water to make tea.
That was an interesting insight about the United States.
There's a lot of challenge.
Immigrants, I think, want to prove themselves.
You know, the data overall, by the way, is immigrant twice as likely to start companies as those who born in America.
Immigrants are four times as likely to win a bell prizes.
Immigrants of the legal variety have a huge influence in the success of this country, obviously.
And so I think about 40% of the founders we back up first generation immigrants.
And you're not burdened by sort of habit when you come here.
You know, I loved rugby as some of you may know.
When I first got here, there were no streaming services.
And so there were no distractions.
I wasn't watching football, I wasn't watching baseball, I wasn't watching anything else.
No rugby was available, so you just studied.
And when you realize that if you don't make it, you're on your way back to where you came from,
where you don't see a great future, it's incredibly motivating.
On the topic of immigrants, as you're graduating, you receive an offer to join PayPal from another immigrant, Elon Musk.
18 months later, you're taking the company public.
Tell us the story of your PayPal journey.
I'm a little bit too certain.
Because I couldn't accept the first offer.
It's actually a classmate of my journey, one of the voices you heard earlier.
Just to illustrate the small network, the 60 degrees of separation, probability.
So Jeremy worked at McKinsey in Australia.
He's younger brother Jonathan also worked at McKinsey.
Both of them did a tour of duty in South Africa.
Jeremy had left just before I joined McKinsey in Johannesburg,
but he's brother Jonathan was there.
Jonathan was my buddy.
There was the person to show me around the office, explained to me how basic things worked.
I get to the GSB and Jeremy's room is opposite mine and Schwab.
We play rugby together.
Obviously we've become good friends.
He was one of the groomsmen at our wedding.
He'd met Elon at city search before he came to the GSB.
I was interested in the company and so he's the one who introduced me to Elon in the first place.
Just to remind you of all these wonderful connections you'll make.
But I couldn't join the company because I was in this F student visa and I didn't have work authorization.
So I turned down the offer and then there was in September, 99.
December 99 he made me a second offer to join and again I had the student visa issue.
And then I got my third offer in March of 2000.
At that point, ex.com, the original ex.com and people were merging and that combination seemed like a fascinating company.
Because it was really about payments and the acceleration of payments in the business captivated me.
And I'd used a couple of projects at the GSB to go and figure out what would I do if I was in this company.
What could be a good business model to make this company work?
I mean something else that happened which is in 1998 there was an emerging market currency crisis.
And long-term capital management blew up and the land that I'd saved in South Africa to come to the GSB lost 30-40% of its value overnight in August of 1998.
And so I didn't have enough money to make it through literally in March of 2000, sitting in your shoes.
March of 2000 I didn't have enough money to quite my share of rent in April.
Now at that point I borrowed money from my former McKinsey mentor who had helped convince me to come to the GSB.
I borrowed money from Hoifon in it was getting embarrassing living had to mouth like that.
I didn't take a summer job after a summer vacation after the graduation.
I started full-time the day after just immediately started to work.
So that was a different environment.
And as I mentioned obviously Hoifon was with me through that difficult decision and that journey of company building.
It was a tough company building journey.
In the summer of 2000, forces were trying to kill us.
eBay was trying to kill us.
The company had a burn rate of $14 million a month in June of 2000.
We had seven months of runway left.
And I remember Mike Merritt showing up at one of our board meetings and the only thing he kept on asking is what you're running away.
And so we had money until January 2001.
And I just kept thinking if we don't figure this out I'm going to rob it back to South Africa.
I got to figure out a business.
And so we started to charge for payments.
We figured out the fraud problem and we kept on scaling the business.
So anyway, Elon left in the summer.
Peter Tiel came back as the CEO at actually interviewed with Peter.
And I still remember some of his interview questions.
So Peter became the CEO and I became VP of finance and risk management at the time.
But I was at all the companies board meetings.
I was involved with fundraising and many of the M&A conversations we had had.
And then in this piece of advice, maybe for all of you.
In 2001, we needed a CFO to take the company public.
And the board endeavored to do an outside search.
And I raised my hand and I said, would you consider me at the time I was 27.
And it was daunting at some level.
But nobody is an authority on your potential like you.
And at some point you can have to raise your hand and not be timid and ask for the opportunity.
Push for the opportunity.
I got that opportunity.
Unfortunately, it didn't make the sufficient number of mistakes between then and the IPO in 2002.
And so we saw that through.
It was an incredible experience.
But in IPO we did a secondary and then we did the acquisition transaction with eBay, which closed later.
I love that quote.
That no one is an authority on your potential but you.
PayPal was a tough environment.
I guess the question that I have is for a lot of a second-year MBA students.
We're in the same boat.
We're thinking about what that next job is going to look like.
How would you think about that and what would you advice me for those of us embarking on that journey?
So there's a one of the insights that I got from Peter actually going back to that interview at PayPal originally.
Peter had this great insight.
Well, firstly, if you interview at a job and most people talk about the vacations, that's a good clue that the job is not that interesting.
But secondly, that most people think one step at a time about their career decisions.
And you really want to expand your time and rise in.
If I had to boil it down to one thing, expand the time and rise in over which you're thinking.
Because if you make a series of relatively short-sighted decisions, you'll make trade-offs that are relatively safe.
And you'll end up in your mid-40s with the mid-life crisis.
So think about that arc of what you can do.
And if you only had one opportunity to make a career decision, maybe you want to be a little more conservative.
And you want to be careful, but that's not the way it is.
You have one choice and another choice and another choice.
And so it may be that the first opportunity out of the GSB is a little risky.
And if it doesn't work out, as long as you work with great people and expand your network and you open up more doors and more opportunities,
the Euro 2 later you've got another interesting opportunity.
And another interesting opportunity.
And when you put that over an arc of a career, you're going to have a lot more fun.
The next fork in your road is when eBay acquires PayPal.
And Meg Whitman actually makes you know after a joint.
You turn that down.
Oh, to stay.
And you turn that down to actually join Sequoia.
And you might call that a pivotal moment, a crucible moment, as you might call it.
What was behind that decision?
How did you make that decision?
So there was a total answer that particular question.
But there were three career decisions I made, each of which sort of versions of the Marshmallow test,
which I'm guessing many of you have heard of because those experiments originally happened here at Bing Noseory School on Stanford campus.
So the first was, I qualified as an actually in South Africa.
It's the highest paid profession in the country.
And I thought that if I worked for McKinsey, it would give me an opportunity to work or to study abroad.
And it took a 50% paper.
And I had to pay back the bursary that I paid for my education to go work at McKinsey.
The second one was to join PayPal where we had to pay back McKinsey for having paid for my tuition here at the GSB.
And my starting salary was I think $80,000 on graduation.
And I go to go with Elon to get more equity and exchange for lowering my cash salary.
So I was earning less than I would have if I'd gone back and I didn't have my debt forgiven.
The third one was when I joined Sequoia.
And at the time, it offered me an incentive package to stay as the CFO paper, which ended up being worth $20 million.
And I turned that down and Sequoia offered me cash salary that matched what I was earning at PayPal at the time with nothing else.
No bonus, no carry, it was a bit on yourself.
And they said, if it works out, you'll do well.
We'll be an interesting career and you'll probably make enough money to not have to worry about it.
But you're going to have to back this off in this situation.
And so, like I said earlier, I had my, you know, the lifeline cool.
Or if I know you should, we can do this and we made that decision.
So it's a co-ed. You've crossed paths as some incredible founders.
Brian Chesky at Airbnb, who was actually here a few years ago.
Larry Page and Sergey Bryn at Google.
Patrick Carson at Stripe. What qualities do you see in founders who changed the world?
I think it starts with authenticity.
A lot of people talk about founder market fit.
The thing I actually look for is founder problem fit.
Why you, the one who has the insight in this particular problem?
Why you so passionate about pursuing this?
And the common thread for our most successful founders is not that they're doing it for the money.
They're just passionate about solving a particular problem.
Something that is often very personal to them, where they have this unique insight,
and they have a burning desire to solve this problem and to go and serve customers.
And when they do and they deliver value, they somehow figure out a good business model to go along with that and they scale.
They will have a growth mindset. They will eager to listen.
Almost all of them, Brian Chesky, for example, an absolute sponge.
The sort of person who listens to advice.
And obviously founders have strong-willed individuals, but they soak up all the benefit of other people's advice and experience.
When I was in first year here at the GSB, one of the professors had this profound insight,
which is that founders are not actually risk takers. They are risk mitigators.
They understand all the variables and go wrong in their business,
and they work feverishly to fix those things and to make sure that the business is on good footing.
So I think that is important. By the way, increasingly we're seeing GSB founders.
So we didn't mention, you know, we invested in a chemical medallia that was the class of 99,
we invested in truly a new bank, the Vid was class of 2012, I believe Tony Shuy was here 2013.
The company may not have heard of yet ethos, which is online life insurance,
or started by Peter and Linky, who were class of 2016.
So there are a lot of really interesting companies coming out of this room.
So that's great to hear that you think the GSB is a good quality.
It's a good quality.
Yeah. You understand business.
It's actually changed quite a lot. I think when I first joined Sequoia, there was a sentiment that,
if I were to spend time on campus looking for investment opportunities for Sequoia,
the GSB may not be the best place to go spend time, and I think it's changed pretty dramatically over the last 20 years.
So just fabulous.
So I want to go back to one point you mentioned, which is that growth mindset.
It's something you've embodied throughout your career.
For years you were famous for writing 10 to the power of 9 on every piece of paper,
to remind yourself you're going to generate $1 billion in return.
In 2020 it was reported that you had generated over $10 billion in gains.
As success change, your mindset in any way?
I think so.
I don't think so.
I think probably would change is more just with time and as you're in a leadership position.
You think more about the performance of your team, you don't just think about your individual contribution.
That's probably the biggest change.
But what are the phrases we have at Sequoia is we're only as good as our next investment.
And a phrase that I've repeated over and over at our all-hands meetings is that nothing will just
as fast as laurels that have been rested on.
And so that's a mindset that I saw when I interviewed Sequoia,
and I didn't interview at any other venture firm, by the way.
At least when I was at the GSB, most of the venture investors came by seem like they were masters of the universe.
And they'd just seem to think too much of themselves.
Let me put it that way.
And at Sequoia, I found something very different.
There was such a humility from people who had enough money to never have to work again,
and yet they were relentless.
They were there early in the mornings.
They were eager to meet the next interesting company that was going to transform the world.
And it was the joy of working with these founders, working with these companies and building them,
that kept them going.
And so I think it's just a very important attribute of what really motivates you.
And at least for us as well, the clients we serve, in a may not be obvious to you,
stand for it as a bit client of Sequoia.
People who's money we invest are almost exclusively in diamonds, foundations and non-profits.
And when you visit our offices, you'll see the names of our limited partners on the doors of our conference rooms.
And so you'll see Stanford, you'll see the welcome trust, you'll see the Dennif Barbarian Institute,
you'll see Boston Children's Hospital, these are the people we work for.
And that's incredibly motivating.
Well, the GSB students, your career seems flawless.
We can see Cinsel and Stanford MBA.
Takes PayPal public a 28.
Sequoia partner year later, and now head of the most iconic venture capital.
Has Rula worth ever failed?
There are 15 times I've made an investment recommendation at Sequoia in my 21, 22 years.
I've recommended an investment that ended up being zero.
When I interviewed a Sequoia, Don Valentine was still alive and interviewed me.
And he actually said that he worried that I hadn't faced enough adversity and failed enough before I joined Sequoia.
Because he was worried that I wouldn't have the fortitude to deal with the adversity of being in our business.
Because the truth is over Sequoia's history over 50 years.
About a half the time we don't generate a profit on an investment.
There's a power load on a millionaire industry and when it works, it works spectacularly.
But you fail a lot.
And that's hard because when I was new shoes, I was used to getting good grades and things kind of just worked.
And that's one of the things that I think you will need to face is you embark on your careers.
Because if you want to be exceptional at something, you're probably going to have to push the boundaries.
And you're going to be at that age of incompetence where sometimes you're not going to get it right.
But if you stay inside that age and that boundary, you'll never do something special or exceptional.
Well, your ability to push yourself is part of the reason for the firm and your predecessors.
Each of whom is actually spoken of you from the top.
Picture to lead the firm.
What most people don't know is that your title is actually Senior Stewart.
What does it mean to you to Stewart Sequoia, can you?
The title of Stewart was very deliberately chosen.
And the mentality we have at Sequoia is that you need to leave the firm in a better place than you found it.
That is our role.
So we have changed the constitution of Sequoia capital to make it virtually impossible to take it public,
but to sell any part of the GP to a third party.
So it's a private partnership to the limit of the law in perpetuity.
And so no generation can sell a piece of Sequoia and profit at the expense of those who came before or those who come after.
And there's a very gradual transition where one generation hands over the reins to a new generation.
And our job is to make sure that this team is well positioned that, you know,
20 or 30 years time Sequoia continues to be one of the leading firms in the world.
And that's what we look for.
And so when I'm interviewing people to join Sequoia, it's not to work for me, it's to work with me.
And I evaluate each candidate wondering, could I imagine you're leading this firm down the road?
Can I imagine you being one of the leading partners making great decisions?
Or I feel proud of what happens at this wonderful partnership after I'm long gone.
Speaking of stewardship, you have been a major patron, a Stanford University.
I think each of us in the room is probably benefited from you in some way.
Because it's right.
Why can't I have jointly done all of that philanthropy at the University?
And on that point, there's actually a building on campus named after both you and Hoyfen here at the GSB.
What has giving back meant to the two of you?
Well, I think you should all recognize that there are many people who came before you who would generous.
And that provided this incredible university, these incredible facilities, provided professureships.
That's what we benefited from.
A lot of generous people that enabled this great institution so that we could study here and we could launch our careers.
And so I think it's with that mindset that we have an obligation also to pay it forward.
I wouldn't be where I am today if it wasn't for Stanford, if it wasn't for the GSB.
It's the same person, same intellect, you know, if I was stuck in Cape Town, I couldn't have done what I've done.
And I wasn't, I wouldn't have had these opportunities had it not been for the GSB saying yes to my application for what I learned here, for professors like, you know,
Professors Cilona who taught me about strategy which infuses things that I do every day at Sequoia.
You know, what I learned, what it meant for me to be here is so important, it just seems natural that you want to give back and pay it forward to an ex generation.
And so we've been delighted to do that both of us here at the GSB and at Lucille.
It brings us to an image joy to be able to give back.
It's really special.
The innovation program was probably the one we liked the most by the way.
So, some people have been interested in that.
And hopefully some good founders for you to back in the future.
There were a couple that are venture funded already, other innovation program, which had an unusual story.
I don't know if you want to talk about that.
Yeah, let's go ahead.
There was in 2020, Dean Leven asked for help from many of the alumni because a lot of the internships for first year students had tried up.
And obviously it's a quite a way to do whatever we can within the portfolio to figure out if the internship opportunities.
But after that call, the Hoifron and I had a conversation about, you know, what's going on at campus and you know, isn't this an awful position.
And certainly, you know, for us as immigrants needing money during, well, at that point I was quite a district as a first year student.
I couldn't have made it through second year if I didn't have a summer job.
There was an income requirement.
And so, as we spoke about it, we came up with this idea to provide essentially a stipend to a certain number of students that summer with the provision that they need to work on something.
They need to work on a business idea to make it somewhat practical.
And so we came back to the school with this idea and we did it at that summer.
And we thought that was it. And then subsequent year a student emailed and said,
I'm doing this program again. I was kind of a strange request.
My COVID was over, you know, vaccines were being rolled out. You know, it didn't make sense.
It's seemingly to need the program still.
And so, I was wondering, I had another conversation about this.
And we wondered, well, well, maybe there's an opportunity for people who don't have the means to be able to pursue something entrepreneurial between first and second year.
Instead of taking a job, just because they needed the money.
And so we said that sounds like an idea.
And we came back to the school and we offered to fund that program for many years going forward.
And obviously the school has done a tremendous job providing the resources to enable students to pursue an entrepreneurial dream.
I spoke to one of the professors here a couple of weeks ago.

It talks about the missing Einstein's, this phenomenon that there's some people who don't do interesting things because they need to work for a living immediately after university or after the education.
I think there's probably some balancing act there where if you don't have a little bit of a pinch, you may be honest, resourceful.
And so the idea of the program is not to be lavish.
I think the amount of money provided per student is probably less than what you could get if you took another ping job.
But it's enough for you to get by. And it's enough for you to pursue an entrepreneurial dream hopefully.
And even if the idea doesn't work out, at least you know, you venture it, you try it.
And hopefully you learn something from the experience.
Well I want to focus for a little bit on what it means to be at the top.
Who are folks you look to for advice or mentors that you look at when you're trying to make a tough decision?
Well firstly, the at the top.
I'm not at the top.
There is no summit. There's only the climb as far as I'm concerned.
